actionable_feedback: Open attempts/baseline/[EXCLUDED_GENERATED_ARTIFACT] in Excel (manual verifier step) and confirm the exact cell formulas for: severity_norm, recency_norm, exposure_norm, priority, portfolio normalized risk, sensitivity perturbation calculations, option combination cost cells, and feasibility booleans.

Fix any hard-coded or transcription errors in option-combination cost cells so each combination cost is computed as SUM(component_cost_cells). Recompute feasibility booleans and update any downstream cells that reference combination cost values.

Add one or more assertion cells in the workbook that validate: combination_cost == SUM(component costs) for each combination, and weight_sum == 1.0. Mark assertion cells clearly (e.g., 'Validation: combination_cost_check').

Append a SensitivitySummary sheet showing +5% and -5% perturbed priority values per record and the resulting binary classification (Needs remediation or Not) so reviewers can immediately see sensitivity outcomes.

Update cio_memo.md to (a) correct the combination-cost numeric typos discovered by the grader, (b) include a short README section with explicit sheet names and key cell/range references (where formulas live), and (c) cite the assertion cells and the SensitivitySummary table.

Link each stated assumption in the memo to the exact workbook cell/range that implements it (e.g., Inputs_Config!B4 for threshold, Records!C2:C5 for expected_pnl_m mapping). Identify which assumptions are critical vs advisory.

Re-run the workbook checks locally after edits and produce an updated memo that states: 'manual workbook verification performed; combination sums validated; assertion cells green' and attach the updated artifacts for re-verification.

If environment reproducibility is required, include a reproducibility note (README) describing how to open the workbook and which cells/sheets to inspect; mention that no macros are used and that the workbook is standard .xlsx.
artifact_specific_comments: tail_hedge_attribution.xlsx: Grader could not parse the workbook in-run (openpyxl unavailable) so please ensure all critical formulas are present in-sheet (not hard-coded) and add assertion cells that make validation obvious (SUM checks for combination costs, weight sum check, threshold cell).
cio_memo.md: Fix the combination-cost transcriptions (e.g., O1+O2, O1+O3, O1+O2+O3 mismatches reported by grader) and include explicit sheet/cell references for the formulas and validation checks.
feedback_summary: Baseline submission meets artifact-contract requirements and produces defensible priority outputs, but verification was limited by runtime inability to parse the .xlsx. Grader found small arithmetic mismatches in memo combination cost sums (off by ~$100 - $1000) and recommends manual workbook inspection to confirm formula-level implementation and correct transcription/sum errors. Also recommend adding explicit cell/range references and a sensitivity summary table to expedite future verification.

observations.sensitivity_claim: No records changed classification under +/-5% perturbation (as stated in memo).

recommended_manual_checks: Open the .xlsx and confirm formulas for normalized metrics, priority, portfolio risk, and sensitivity perturbations.
Validate combination-cost assertion cells and update memo numbers where necessary.

verifier_issues.summary: Workbook parsing not available in runtime (openpyxl missing); manual verifier must open the .xlsx to confirm formulas.

verifier_issues.recommended_action: Fix SUM formulas / transcription errors and re-run checks.
verifier_issues.summary: Minor arithmetic mismatches between memo-listed combination costs and exact sums.

raw_llm_output.suggested_revision: Correct the small arithmetic/transcription errors in option-combination cost cells, add explicit cell references and assertion/validation cells in the workbook, append a SensitivitySummary table (+/-5%), and update the CIO memo (README, corrected numbers, links to cells). After these local fixes, re-run verification and attach updated artifacts.
trace_specific_comments: Trace shows an attempted Python/openpyxl workbook build failed due to ModuleNotFoundError ('No module named openpyxl'). The worker used a fallback serialization approach to produce the .xlsx but the runtime preview lacked parsing capability. Because of this, the grader relied on the memo and workbook metadata rather than direct cell-level inspection - manual opening of the workbook is required to confirm formulas.

Include in the revised attempt a short verification note describing how the workbook was generated (toolchain) and confirming that formulas are native Excel formulas (no macros) so verifiers know how to inspect cells.
